How to bake bread

Baking bread is one of the great pleasures of cooking. Start with flour, water, salt, and yeast.

Mix the ingredients in a bowl. Knead for ten minutes. Let rest for an hour.

Bake at 450 degrees for 30 minutes. Cool before slicing.

Once you master basic bread, you can experiment with different flours, hydration ratios, and fermentation times. Sourdough offers deeper flavor, while whole wheat adds nutrition and earthiness. The key is understanding how gluten develops and how time and temperature interact.

Professional bakers often use baker's percentages to scale recipes precisely. A basic formula: 100% flour, 65-75% water, 1-2% salt, 0.5-1% yeast. Adjust based on desired crumb structure and flavor development.

For best results, use bread flour or all-purpose flour with higher protein content. Store in a cool, dry place. Once baked, cool completely before slicing to avoid a gummy interior. The crust should be golden brown and crispy.

Temperature control is crucial for quality results. Room temperature fermentation develops flavor over hours, while cold fermentation in the refrigerator can extend this process over days. Many advanced bakers prefer cold fermentation for improved taste and texture.

The hydration level determines crumb structure. Lower hydration yields tighter, denser crumb. Higher hydration creates open, irregular crumb with larger holes. Most artisan breads fall in the 75-80% range.

Once you understand these fundamentals, you can troubleshoot common problems like dense crumb, pale crust, or excessive spreading. Each variable affects the final product in measurable ways.

Start your baking journey today with simple ingredients and patience. Great bread requires time and attention, but the rewards are worth the effort. Happy baking!

Advanced Techniques

For those seeking greater mastery, consider exploring laminated doughs, preferments like poolish and biga, and specialty techniques. These methods unlock new flavors and textures. Levain breads benefit from extended fermentation.

Shaping technique matters significantly. Tension in the dough helps with oven spring and determines the final height. Loose shaping yields flat, spread-out loaves. Tight shaping creates taller, more structured loaves with excellent rise.
